news_list.title: Full reportTrump says US will blockade strait of Hormuz after Iran peace talks fail
news_list.content:
Iran warns move would breach ceasefire as US president also repeats threats to strike critical infrastructure

Middle East crisis 鈥?live updates

Donald Trump has said the US will begin blockading the strait of Hormuz in an attempt to take control of the strategic waterway from Iran in the aftermath offailed peace negotiationsbetween the countries in Pakistan.

The US president also threatened to bomb Iran鈥檚 water treatment facilities, power plants and bridges if Tehran did not agree to abandon its nuclear weapons programme, the key sticking point between the two sides.

Trump鈥檚 surprise announcement of a blockade came after face-to-face peace negotiations between the US and Iran in Islamabad thatlasted 21 hourscollapsed on Sunday morning.

JD Vance, the vice-president and head of the US team, said Iran had refused to give up the possibility of developing nuclear weapons, while the Iranian delegates said Washington needed to do more to win their trust.

Risking another increase in oil prices, Trump said he had instructed the US navy to begin 鈥渂lockading any and all ships trying to enter, or leave, the strait of Hormuz,鈥?and he accused Iran of extortion with its own scheme of charging tolls to tankers. US Central Command announced the blockade would begin on Monday at 10am ET (2pm GMT).

Iran鈥檚 Revolutionary Guards responded by declaring that if any warships approached the strait to enforce a blockade 鈥?usually considered an act of war 鈥?it would be considered a breach of the current ceasefire and would be dealt with strongly. They insisted the strait remained under Iranian control.

Two US destroyers crossed and recrossed the strait without incident on Saturday, although Iranian media said they were threatened as they left. The US military said it was the start of a mine-clearance mission.

Trump said US warships would 鈥渟eek and interdict every vessel鈥?that had paid Iran since the start of the conflict and begin de-mining the central section of the strait, previously declared a 鈥渉azardous area鈥?by Tehran, although it is unclear how many mines have been laid.

About 100 tankers have transited the strait since the US and Israel started bombing Iran, paying up to $2m each time for passage. Many were bound for China and India, carrying Iranian oil products, and chasing them down could complicate relations between the US and the importing nations.

Oil prices rose in early market after Trump鈥檚 announcement of a blockade. The price of US crude oil rose 8% to $104.24 a barrel and Brent crude oil, the international standard, rose 7% to $102.29.

The US and Iranian delegations left Pakistan soon after the talks ended. Vance said he spoke with Trump at least half a dozen times during the talks, held during a 14-day ceasefire announced by the US, Israel and Iran overnight on 7 and 8 April.

鈥淲e need to see an affirmative commitment that [Iran] will not seek a nuclear weapon, and they will not seek the tools that would enable them to quickly achieve a nuclear weapon,鈥?Vance said. 鈥淭hat is the core goal of the president of the United States, and that鈥檚 what we鈥檝e tried to achieve through these negotiations.鈥漒n
Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, the speaker of the Iranian parliament, who led Tehran鈥檚 negotiators, said he and his colleagues had offered 鈥渃onstructive initiatives鈥?while the US had been 鈥渦nable to gain the trust of the Iranian delegation in this round of negotiations鈥?

Iran鈥檚 semi-official Tasnim news agency said 鈥渆xcessive鈥?US demands had hindered reaching an agreement, and the foreign ministry said more time was needed. 鈥淣aturally, from the beginning we should not have expected to reach an agreement in a single session,鈥?the ministry鈥檚 spokesperson Esmail Baghaei said, according to the state broadcaster IRIB.

Pakistani mediators called on the US and Iran to refrain from renewing hostilities and said they would try to arrange a fresh round of talks. 鈥淚t is imperative that the parties continue to uphold their commitment to the ceasefire,鈥?said Pakistan鈥檚 foreign minister, Ishaq Dar.

Vance was accompanied by the US special envoy Steve Witkoff and Trump鈥檚 son-in-law Jared Kushner. They met Ghalibaf and the Iranian foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, for several negotiating sessions at the Serena hotel in Islamabad, with Pakistan鈥檚 army chief, Asim Munir, also present.

Iran鈥檚 delegation arrived on Friday dressed in black in mourning for the late supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and others killed in the war. They carried shoes and bags of children killed during the bombing of a school next to a military compound, the Iranian government said.

A Pakistani source said the discussions, the highest-level direct contact between the US and Iran since 1979, were unpredictable in tone. 鈥淭here were mood swings from the two sides, and the temperature went up and down during the meeting,鈥?a Pakistani source said after the first round.

In a Fox News interview on Sunday, Trump threatened to restart the bombing of Iran if a deal could not be agreed, and threatened to target the country鈥檚 water supply as well as its bridges and power generation.

Trump said: 鈥淭he only thing left, really, is their water, which would be very devastating to hit. I would hate to do it, but it鈥檚 their water, their desalinisation plans, their electric generating plants, which are very easy to hit.鈥漒n
The president was asked if gas and oil prices might be lower by the US midterms in November, an indication that attacking Iran was not an economic mistake. Prices 鈥渃ould be the same or maybe a little bit higher,鈥?a non-committal Trump replied.

In southern Lebanon, at least 11 people were killed and there were at least 30 Israeli strikes in the region, the country鈥檚 state news service said.

At the Vatican, Pope Leo XIV called for a ceasefire after his Sunday prayers and said he felt 鈥渃loser than ever鈥?to Lebanon鈥檚 people.

The war, which began with US and Israeli strikes on Iran six weeks ago, has killed at least 3,000 people in Iran, 2,020 in Lebanon, 23 in Israel and more than a dozen in Gulf Arab states. It has caused lasting damage to infrastructure in half a dozen Middle Eastern countries.

news_list.title: ExplainerWhy is the US threatening to block Iranian ports and how would it work?
news_list.content:
Announcement has left markets in yet more uncertainty, with questions over how blockade will be enforced

Middle East crisis 鈥?live updates

The US military has announced it will enforce a blockade on all Iranian ports in the strait of Hormuz from Monday after ceasefire talks with Iran over the weekendended without an agreement.

The strait has emerged as Iran鈥檚 most effective weapon in its asymmetric war with the US, which alongside Israel, launched the now six-week conflict in the Middle East. The US-Israeli attacks caused significant damage and loss of life but failed to topple Tehran鈥檚 regime or end its nuclear ambitions. They have instead resulted in a global economic crisis asIranin effect closed the vital waterway, through which about 20% of the world鈥檚 oil and gas supply previously moved. The consequent rise in the oil price has fuelled fears of damaging inflation while exports of other vital goods, such as fertilisers required for food production, have collapsed.

Donald Trump has been left scrambling to respond to the fallout, and on Sundaythreatened to impose his own blockade鈥?a move that pushed global markets in another period of deep uncertainty.

On Sunday, the president posted to social media: 鈥淭he United States Navy, the Finest in the World, will begin the process of BLOCKADING any and all Ships trying to enter, or leave, the Strait of Hormuz.鈥漒n
Accusing Iran of 鈥淲ORLD EXTORTION鈥? Trump threatened that any person who attacked the US vessels would be 鈥淏LOWN TO HELL!鈥漒n
Trump鈥檚 sweeping threat to blockade 鈥渁ny and all鈥?ships was later contradicted by his own military, which announced a more targeted blockade. The US Central Command (Centcom) said the blockade would be confined to vessels transiting through Iranian ports 鈥?and that it would permit passage of ships headed to ports belonging to the US鈥檚 Gulf allies. Centcom said the blockade would come into effect at 10am ET (3pm BST) and apply to the Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, which are connected by the thin strait of Hormuz.

Creating further confusion, the US military later sent a note to seafarers saying the blockade would only be enforced in waters east of the strait of Hormuz, according to Reuters.

Trump told Fox News that allies, many of whom he has criticised for failing to back the war, wanted to help with the operation in the strait. The British prime minister, Keir Starmer, said London would not support the blockade and that despite 鈥渃onsiderable pressure鈥? he would not get 鈥渄ragged into the war鈥? The Australian prime minister, Anthony Albanese, said his country was not asked to participate, while the Spanish defence minister, Margarita Robles, said a US blockade 鈥渕akes no sense鈥?

Reports indicate that the reopening of the strait 鈥?which was open before the war 鈥?was one of the big sticking points in theweekend negotiations between the US and Iran. Tehran has indicated that it would like to retain control of the waterway after the war has ended, and has floated a plan to charge a fee of up to $2m for each ship that passes through the waterway. Trump and other world leaders have rejected such a plan as an attack on 鈥渇reedom of navigation鈥?

Despite Trump鈥檚 claims that reopening the waterway is not his responsibility, the president is under pressure to resolve the issue before the continued closure of the strait unleashes an even greater crisis for the global economy. Trump鈥檚 envoys failed to negotiate effectively with Tehran to reopen the waterway.

The US military has not offered many details yet, including how many warships will enforce it, whether warplanes will be used and whether any Gulf allies will assist in the effort.

Experts say it is unlikely the US military would fire missiles or other weapons at tankers, given the risk of an environmental disaster. The most likely option is the US navy will try to force vessels to change course through threats, and if that doesn鈥檛 work, they will launch armed boarding parties to take physical control of the ships, experts say.

鈥淭rump wants a quick fix. The reality is, this mission is difficult to execute alone and likely unsustainable over the medium to long term,鈥?said Dana Stroul, a former senior Pentagon official during the Biden administration now at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy.

Any US naval presence in areas close to Iran would also expose the military to potential attacks.

Experts say the blockadecould lead to higher oil pricesbut much depends on its 鈥渟cope and implementation.鈥漒n
Kevin Book, the managing director of research at the research firm ClearView Energy Partners, said that leaner volumes generally mean tighter markets and higher prices. 鈥淗ow Tehran responds matters, too. Iranian and/or Houthi reprisals against Gulf producers鈥?alternative routes could drive prices still higher,鈥?Book said.

By closing the strait to vessels carrying Iranian oil, Trump could cut off one of the regime鈥檚 major sources of funding 鈥?but it could also have a negative effect on global prices.

About 100 tankers have transited the strait since the US and Israel started bombing Iran, most of them carrying Iranian oil products bound for China and India. The US has allowed Iran to continue these exports 鈥?and evenlifted sanctions on Iranian oil at sea鈥?in a move to ease supply pressures.

After Trump鈥檚 announcement, the price of US crude increased 8% to $104.24 a barrel and Brent crude oil rose 7% to $102.29. Brent crude, the international standard, has gone from roughly $70 a barrel before the war in late February to as high as $119 over the course of the conflict.

Iran鈥檚 Revolutionary Guards have said any warships that approach the strait to enforce a blockade will be considered in breach of the current ceasefire and be dealt with strongly. They said the strait remained under Iranian control.

Trump floated the possibility on Sunday of a resumption of US strikes inside Iran, citing missile factories as one possible target.

news_list.title: USDonald Trump launches extraordinary attack on Pope Leo calling him 鈥榳eak鈥?and 鈥榯errible鈥?,
: null,
UKUK will not join any Trump blockade of strait of Hormuz
news_list.content:
UK鈥檚 willingness to consider role in removing mines from strait is seen as distinct from Trump鈥檚 blockade proposal

Middle East crisis 鈥?live updates

The UK will not be involved in any blockade of the strait of Hormuz, the Guardian understands, afterclaims by Donald Trumpon Sunday that the US would be blockading the waterway with the assistance of Nato allies.

Speaking to Fox News, Trump said 鈥渋t won鈥檛 take long to clean out the strait鈥?and claimed 鈥渘umerous countries are going to be helping us鈥? adding that the UK and other nations were sending minesweepers.

The UK has previouslysuggested it could play a rolein making the strait of Hormuz safe to pass, and it has mine-hunting systems and anti-drone capabilities already in the region.

But there have beenconcerns in Whitehallthat complying with Trump鈥檚 demand to send ships could escalate the crisis. The UK鈥檚 willingness to consider a role in mine-removal operations is seen as distinct from Trump鈥檚 blockade proposal.

A government spokesperson said the UK would continue to 鈥渟upport freedom of navigation and the opening of the strait of Hormuz, which is urgently needed to support the global economy and the cost of living back home鈥?

They added: 鈥淭he strait of Hormuz must not be subject to tolling. We are urgently working with France and other partners to put together a wide coalition to protect freedom of navigation.鈥漒n
Starmer has previously said talks were ongoing between allies in Europe, the Gulf partners and the US to create a 鈥渃redible, viable plan鈥?for reopening the strait after Iran blocked traffic.

Trump鈥檚 comments around a blockade of the strait of Hormuz came hours after Wes Streeting criticised his rhetoric on Iran as 鈥渋ncendiary, provocative and outrageous鈥? On Sunday Trump again hit out at the UK鈥檚 actions during the conflict and repeated a jibe that appeared to compare Starmer toNeville Chamberlain.

鈥淢r Starmer said we鈥檒l send the equipment after the war is over,鈥?Trump told Fox, saying those words were a 鈥淣eville Chamberlain-type statement鈥?

It is not the first time Trump has appeared to compare Starmer to Chamberlain, long criticised for following a policy of appeasement toward Adolf Hitler. Starmer has not responded to the US president鈥檚 jibes specifically. Before Trump鈥檚 comments on Sunday, Starmer had urged the US and Iran 鈥渢o find a way through鈥?after thefailure of peace talksover the weekend.

The prime minister also called for the ceasefire to continue after having a conversation with the sultan of Oman, Haitham bin Tariq Al Said, on Sunday morning.

The fragiletwo-week truceannounced last Tuesday was thrown into uncertainty after Washington and Tehran鈥檚 21 hours of negotiations ended without agreement being reached in the early hours of Sunday. The US vice-president, JD Vance, who was in Pakistan for the talks,said Iran鈥檚 refusalto commit to not building a nuclear weapon was the reason for the lack of a deal.

Streeting called the failure of US-Iran peace talks disappointing and said the success of future negotiations was necessary 鈥渋n all of our interests鈥?

鈥淎s ever in diplomacy, you鈥檙e failing until you succeed,鈥?the health secretary told Sunday Morning With Trevor Phillips on Sky News. 鈥淪o while these talks may not have ended in success, that doesn鈥檛 mean there isn鈥檛 merit in continuing to try.鈥漒n
He acknowledged it had been a 鈥渄ifficult few months鈥?for the relationship between the UK and the US. 鈥淥n so many other things, our interests as the UK and the US are intertwined. We are old and close friends and we鈥檝e got a shared outlook as democratic countries and we鈥檝e got shared security interests,鈥漢e said.

Asked about the rhetoric used by Trump, who last week warned Iran that a 鈥渨hole civilisation will die鈥?if it did not meet his demands, Streeting said: 鈥淥ver the course of the last week, President Trump has said some pretty bold 鈥?in Yes Minister language 鈥?incendiary, provocative, outrageous things on social media. I think we鈥檝e all come to learn that you judge President Trump through what he does, not just what he says.鈥漒n
Rachel Reeves said again on Sunday that the war in Iran would 鈥渃ome at a cost to British families and businesses鈥? Writing in the Sunday Times,the chancellor said: 鈥淭hese are not costs I wanted, but they are costs we will have to respond to. As chancellor, I have vowed that my economic approach to this crisis will be both responsive to a changing world and responsible in the national interest.鈥漒n
Streeting praised Starmer鈥檚 鈥済rit and guts鈥?in declining to support initial US-Israeli strikes on Iran. 鈥淭here are few former British prime ministers alive today who would have made the same judgment call that Keir made on not joining the war in Iran,鈥?he said.

news_list.title: Viktor Orb谩n delivers a concession speech in BudapestBoys climb a traffic light holding Tisza signs during celebrations in BudapestDowncast Fidesz supporters watch Orb谩n鈥檚 speech on a screenCrowds in Budapest celebrate after partial election results are announcedPeople celebrate the election result in central BudapestP茅ter Magyar addresses supporters in Budapest after his victoryP茅ter Magyar addresses supporters in Budapest after his victoryP茅ter Magyar gives a speech
news_list.content:
After some 20 minutes,Magyaris turning towards foreign policy now.

Hesays the Hungarians are proud to be a part of the EU and Nato,even as he acknowledges some of the EU鈥檚 flaws with its networks of lobbies and interests.

鈥淚t鈥檚 a complicated bureaucratic, compromise-seeking 鈥?organisation,鈥?he says.

But he insists 鈥測ou can find compromises鈥?that will work forHungary.

鈥淚 am sure we will have debates 鈥?but we are not going there to fight for the sake of fightingso we can write on billboards that Brussels is evil and needs to be stopped,鈥?he says in another swipe at Orb谩n.

Again:expect him to get asked about what these compromises on key issues鈥?such as the rule of law, migration andUkraine鈥揷ould look likewhen we eventually get to Q&A.

news_list.title: ExplainedWhat does P茅ter Magyar鈥檚 win in Hungary mean for the EU and Ukraine?
news_list.content:
Tisza leader has promised to repair Budapest鈥檚 ties with Brussels after years of strained relations under Viktor Orb谩n

Europe live 鈥?latest updates

P茅ter Magyar鈥檚resounding election victorybrings an end to 16 years of rightwing populist rule under Viktor Orb谩n. But the effects of that win are likely to be felt well beyond Hungary鈥檚 borders, from Brussels to Moscow and from Kyiv to Washington.

Orb谩n鈥檚 frequent efforts to undermine the EU鈥檚 attempts at collective action 鈥?not to mention his proud defence of the 鈥渋lliberal democracy鈥?that Hungary became on his watch 鈥?exasperated and infuriated the European Commission. His vetoing of a massive EU loan to Ukraine and his strategy of delaying, and sometimes opposing, EU sanctions on Russia, left the bloc looking toothless and divided.

Meanwhile, his contempt for democratic norms, failure to tackle corruption,disdain for the rule of lawand hiscrackdowns on the medialed the commission to suspend billions of euros in funding to Hungary.

Magyar, on the other hand, has promised to repair Hungary鈥檚 strained relationship with the bloc and to address many of its concerns. In a speech on Monday afternoon, he struck a conciliatory note, saying that while the EU was 鈥渁 complicated bureaucratic, compromise-seeking 鈥?organisation鈥? he felt compromises could be made. 鈥淚 am sure we will have debates,鈥?he added. 鈥淏ut we are not going there to fight for the sake of fighting聽so we can write on billboards that Brussels is evil and needs to be stopped.鈥漒n
Ursula von der Leyen, the president of the European Commission, made plain her relief at the election result 鈥?and her hopes for rebooted relations with Hungary 鈥?in a post on X. 鈥淗ungary has chosen Europe,鈥?she wrote. 鈥淓urope has always chosen Hungary. Together, we are stronger. A country returns to its European path. The Union grows stronger.鈥漒n
Magyar鈥檚 predecessor made no secret of his proximity to Moscow. Not only did Orb谩n continue buying Russian oil and gas 鈥?and meeting Vladimir Putin after Russia鈥檚 full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022 鈥?he alsoblocked a vital 鈧?0bn (拢78bn) EU loan to Ukrainefor urgently needed military aid and government support. Recent media reports have also suggested that Budapestshared confidential EU informationwith the Kremlin, prompting further fury from the EU.

Although Magyar described Moscow as a 鈥渟ecurity risk鈥?for Europe in Monday鈥檚 speech 鈥?and insisted he would tell Putin 鈥渋t would be nice to end the killing after four years鈥?were he to call 鈥?some of his lines on Russia and Ukraine are not wildly divergent from Orb谩n鈥檚. He said he hoped the Russian aggression would end soon so that sanctions against Russia could be lifted, adding: 鈥淲e are neighbours to Russia and it is not in Europe鈥檚 interests to buy raw materials at higher prices because that destroys our competitiveness.鈥漒n
While Magyar said his government would not support Ukraine鈥檚 fast-track entry to the EU, he did crucially signal that he was happy with the 鈧?0bn EU loan deal to Kyiv, raising hopes the money could finally be disbursed. But he stressed the need for the opt-out that exempts Hungary from contributing to the loan, saying his country was in a 鈥?very difficult financial situation鈥?

The Kremlin has said it hopes to 鈥渃ontinue pragmatic contacts with the new leadership鈥? while Ukraine鈥檚 president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy, said his country was 鈥渞eady for meetings and joint constructive work for the benefit of both nations, as well as peace, security and stability in Europe鈥?

The outgoing prime minister鈥檚 loud and aggressive defence of traditional Christian family values made him a hugely popular and potent figurehead for his rightwing and far-right contemporaries.

While his critics saw him as illiberal, intolerant and profoundly undemocratic, his admirers delighted in his scorn for many of the values held dear across the majority of EU countries. Orb谩n鈥檚 departure has left them mourning a much-cherished totem.

The French far-right leader Marine Le Pen said Orb谩n had 鈥渄efended Hungary鈥檚 freedom and sovereignty with courage and determination鈥? while Italy鈥檚 pragmatic prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, congratulated Magyar but also thanked Orb谩n for 鈥渋ntense collaboration for these years鈥?

Santiago Abascal, the leader of Spain鈥檚 far-right Vox party, was less measured. 鈥淗ungary was the only European nation that was safe from the Islamist invasion,鈥?he wrote on X on Sunday night. 鈥淭he defeat of Victor Orb谩n puts that in danger. Orb谩n leaves a Hungary that is much better than the one he inherited. And he leaves a deep mark on all the patriotic forces of Europe.鈥漒n
Orb谩n鈥檚 defeat will also come as a blow to Donald Trump, his administration and the Maga movement. As Trump鈥檚 former adviser Steve Bannon once noted, Orb谩n was 鈥淭rump before Trump鈥?鈥?a beguiling, one-man blueprint for illiberal, bulldozer politics. The US president had repeatedly endorsed Orb谩n and promised, three days ago, to bring US 鈥渆conomic might鈥?to the country if Orb谩n was re-elected.

Orb谩n鈥檚 political and ideological importance to Trump was further underlined by last week鈥檚 visit by the US vice-president, JD Vance. 鈥淲e had to show that there are actually lots of friends across the world who recognise that Viktor and his government are doing a good job and they鈥檙e important partners for peace,鈥?Vance saidas he attempted to brush off accusations of US electoral interference.

The scale of Magyar鈥檚 victory has already prompted some on the European right to ponder whether a Trump endorsement could actually be the kiss of death. Belgium鈥檚 defence minister, Theo Francken, described Vance鈥檚 support as鈥渁 really dumb campaign move鈥? adding: 鈥淚鈥檓 a rightwing politician and think the far right is being really stupid. And that the Magas should really stop campaigning internationally because everyone and everything they support loses the elections. Lead instead of gold. Best advertisement for the left. Crazy.鈥漒n
Unsurprisingly, Magyar described the US on Monday as a very important partner and one with whomHungaryneeds good relations. He added that while he wouldn鈥檛 call Trump, he would speak to him if the White House phoned.

news_list.title: HungaryHungarian opposition ousts Viktor Orb谩n after 16 years in power
news_list.content:
P茅ter Magyar鈥檚 Tisza party wins election as prime minister concedes defeat, in result likely to reshape ties with EU

Magyar set to outline Hungary plans 鈥揈urope live

鈥楬ungary has chosen Europe鈥? EU leaders jubilant after Magyar鈥檚 victory

Hungary鈥檚 opposition Tisza party, led byP茅ter Magyar, has won the election, bringing an end to Viktor Orb谩n鈥檚 16-year grip on power, in a result that is likely to rattle the White House and reshape the country鈥檚 relationship with the EU.

Less than three hours after polls closed on Sunday, Orb谩n conceded defeat after what he described as a 鈥減ainful but unambiguous鈥?election result.

鈥淚 congratulated the victorious party,鈥?the rightwing populist told supporters in Budapest. 鈥淲e are going to serve the Hungarian nation and our homeland from opposition as well.鈥漒n
With 98.74% of the vote counted, Magyar鈥檚 Tisza party was projected to have won 138 of the 199 seats in the country鈥檚 parliament, giving them a super-majority capable of amending the constitution and key laws, suggesting they would be able to reverse some of the changes made by Orb谩n and Fidesz, and potentially unlock EU funds.

Fidesz won 55 seats, while the extreme-right Mi Haz谩nk party won six.

Magyar, who pledged to repair Hungary鈥檚 strained relationship with the EU, crack down on corruption and funnel funds towards long-neglected public services, said Tisza voters had rewritten Hungarian history.

鈥淢y fellow Hungarians, we have done it!,鈥?he told the tens of thousands of jubilant supporters who had gathered along the banks of the Danube river to celebrate.

鈥淭onight, truth prevailed over lies,鈥?said Magyar, 45. 鈥淭oday, we won because Hungarians didn鈥檛 ask what their homeland could do for them 鈥?they asked what they could do for their homeland. You found the answer. And you followed through.鈥漒n
The election was being closely watched around the world as a test of the resilience of the Maga movement and the global far right, many of whomhave long lookedto Orb谩n as an inspiration and sought to follow his playbook.

Days before the election, JD Vance had travelled to Budapest, with the US vice-presidentsaying that hehad come to 鈥渉elp鈥?Orb谩n. Donald Trump had also repeatedly endorsed Orb谩n, most recently on Friday when he vowed to bring US 鈥渆conomic might鈥?to the country if Orb谩n was re-elected.

US Democrat Hakeem Jeffries, the US House of Representatives minority leader, said the results of the Hungarian election did not bode well for the Trump administration. 鈥淔ar-right authoritarianViktor Orb谩nhas lost the election,鈥?he wrote on social media. 鈥淭rump sycophants and Maga extremists in Congress are up next in November. Winter is coming.鈥漒n
In recent months, Orb谩n,62, had alsobeen endorsedby rightwing and far-right leaders ranging from France鈥檚 Marine Le Pen to Italy鈥檚 Giorgia Meloni and Israel鈥檚 Benjamin Netanyahu.

Meloni was among those who congratulated Magyar on Sunday night, promising her country鈥檚 continued cooperation and wishing him success. She added: 鈥淚 thank my friend Viktor Orb谩n for the intense collaboration over the years, and I know that he will continue to serve his nation, also from the opposition.鈥漒n
Leaders from acrossEuropehailed the result, with Keir Starmer describing it as a 鈥渉istoric moment, not only for Hungary, but for European democracy鈥?on social media.

In recent weeks, the antagonistic relationship between Orb谩n鈥檚 government and the EU had plunged to new lows,after Orb谩n vetoedfurther EU sanctions on Russia, as well as an additional 鈧?0bn (拢78bn) loan for Ukraine. Tensions between Budapest and Brusselshad since boiled over, following allegations that Orb谩n鈥檚 government had shared confidential EU information with Moscow.

News of the change in government prompted an outpouring of response from across the EU. 鈥淓urope鈥檚 heart is beating stronger in Hungary tonight,鈥?said Ursula von der Leyen, the president of the European Commission. 鈥淎 country reclaims its European path. The Union grows stronger.鈥漒n
Donald Tusk, Poland鈥檚 prime minister, welcomed Magyar with a jibe at Orb谩n. 鈥淏ack together! Glorious victory, dear friends!鈥?he posted on social media, adding in Hungarian: 鈥淩ussians, go home!鈥漒n
Emmanuel Macron, France鈥檚 president, said he had spoken with Magyar to congratulate him, while the German chancellor, Friedrich Merz, said he was 鈥渓ooking forward鈥?to working with Magyar.

From Ukraine, Volodymyr Zelenskyy said the country was ready to advance our cooperation with Hungary, even as Magyar has said he would continue Orb谩n鈥檚 opposition to sending arms to the country and fast-tracking EU entry for Kyiv. 鈥淲e are ready for meetings and joint constructive work for the benefit of both nations, as well as peace, security, and stability in Europe.鈥漒n
As Magyar crisscrossed Hungary during the campaign, holding as many as six rallies a day, Orb谩n had consistently trailed in the polls, suggesting the election was poised to end his efforts to transformHungaryinto a 鈥減etri dish for illiberalism鈥?

His rightwing populist government used its time in office tosteadily whittle awayat the checks and balances that constrained its power: rewriting election laws to its own benefit, manoeuvring to put loyalists in control of an estimated 80% of the country鈥檚 media, and retooling the country鈥檚 judiciary.

Ahead of the election, as the governmentstepped up its effortsto clamp down on dissent, local resistance swelled, bursting into public view as hundreds of thousands of peopletook to the streetsof Budapest in June in defiance of the government鈥檚 efforts to ban Pride.

Sunday鈥檚 election saw a record turnout of nearly 80%, according to the National Election Office. The result was likely due, in part, to the massive number of youths who mobilised against Orb谩n.One pollsuggested that as many as 65% of voters under the age of 30 鈥?many of whom had come of age as the countryplunged in press freedom rankings, was accused of being an 鈥渆lectoral autocracy鈥? andbecame the most corrupt country in the EU鈥?were planning to cast their vote against Orb谩n.

On Sunday evening, many of them thronged the banks of the Danube, chanting 鈥淲e did it!鈥?while others chanted 鈥淚t鈥檚 over!鈥?as they made their way through the city鈥檚 metro stations.

鈥淭he dictatorship, rightwing ideology and all of that will disappear now, and we have a chance for a better country,鈥?said N贸ri, 24, as tears welled in her eyes. 鈥淚鈥檓 feeling hopeful and happy.鈥漒n
